schools.summary: Gill: the people being numbered and the land to be divided, the Lord thought proper to renew the laws concerning sacrifices, the more needful as it was now thirty-eight years since they were first given, to be observed when they came into Canaan.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the laws of public sacrifice renewed
worship set in order before the land

schools.summary: Gill: these concerned the whole congregation, not private persons; they might be seen not merely as commands but as tokens of God's favour, that despite all their rebellions he would still accept their offerings, to be offered in their due season.

themes: My offering, and my bread for my sacrifices made by fire, for a sweet savour
God's gracious acceptance, not his need
Christ a sweetsmelling savour to God (Eph. 5:2)

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord orders the sacrifices particularly and distinctly, this being (Jarchi) an admonition to the sanhedrim to see them offered.
Keil & Delitzsch: two lambs of the first year without spot, day by day, for a continual burnt offering.

themes: two lambs of the first year without spot day by day, for a continual burnt offering
the foundation of Israel's worship
Christ the Lamb, his offering ever-availing (Heb. 10:14)

schools.summary: Gill: the morning lamb was to make atonement, as the Targum says, for the sins of the night.
Keil & Delitzsch: the one lamb in the morning and the other at even.

themes: The one lamb shalt thou offer in the morning, and the other lamb... at even
the day bounded in sacrifice
life kept under the covering of the Lamb

schools.summary: Gill: the meat offering always went along with the burnt offering.
Ellicott: the 'beaten oil' is the pure oil for the light and the offerings (Exod. 27:20).

themes: a tenth part of an ephah of flour for a meat offering, mingled with... beaten oil
the field's fruit joined to the lamb
the oil of the Spirit breathing through the gift

schools.summary: Gill: the meat offering was burnt as well as the lambs, at least part of it.
Ellicott: 'ordained' is 'made in Mount Sinai'; Ibn Ezra infers the Israelites ceased to offer burnt sacrifices through the wandering.

themes: It is a continual burnt offering, which was ordained in mount Sinai for a sweet savour
the daily sacrifice given with the law
never to cease, the heart of Israel's worship

schools.summary: Gill: the drink offering of wine, strong wine, the fourth part of a hin, went with the meat offering for the morning lamb.
Clarke: the word for 'strong wine' (sikera); see Lev. 10:9.

themes: the drink offering thereof... the strong wine to be poured unto the LORD
the libation completing the daily sacrifice
the self-poured life and the blood of Christ (Phil. 2:17)

schools.summary: Gill: the other lamb was offered at even, as before directed.
Keil & Delitzsch: the evening lamb with the same meat and drink offerings as the morning, a sweet savour unto the LORD.

themes: the other lamb... at even... a sacrifice made by fire, of a sweet savour
the evening answering the morning exactly
the worship of God constant, day and night

schools.summary: Gill: these were just such lambs as appointed for the daily sacrifice.
Henry: the new moons and sabbaths are often spoken of together as great solemnities, comfortable to the saints and typical of gospel grace; every sabbath the offering was doubled.

themes: on the sabbath day two lambs of the first year without spot
the daily offering doubled on the holier day
typical of gospel grace, the rest in Christ

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: the burnt offering of one sabbath was not to be offered on another but only on its own; every sacrifice in its own season.
Keil & Delitzsch: this is the burnt offering of every sabbath, beside the continual burnt offering.

themes: This is the burnt offering of every sabbath, beside the continual burnt offering
the special day added, never instead
the continual offering the foundation of all

schools.summary: Gill: the new moon was religiously observed, marked by the blowing of trumpets over the sacrifices, attendance on the word of the Lord, and abstinence from worldly business.
Ellicott: in the beginnings of the months a burnt offering was offered — two bullocks, one ram, seven lambs.

themes: in the beginnings of your months... two young bullocks, and one ram, seven lambs
each month begun with sacrifice
the calendar of Israel's life sanctified by the altar

schools.summary: Gill: the quantities of flour for each bullock, ram, and lamb are the same as elsewhere, the oil half a hin for a bullock, a third for a ram, a fourth for a lamb, with proportioned drink offerings.

themes: three tenth deals of fine flour... for one bullock; and two tenth deals... for one ram
each meat offering graded to its victim
order in worship, each gift measured to its place

schools.summary: Gill: this was a sin offering, typical of Christ who was made an offering for sin, of the kind that were eaten; even the festival needed a sin offering, Christ taking away the sin of our holy things as well as other sins.

themes: one kid of the goats for a sin offering unto the LORD... beside the continual burnt offering
even the festival needing atonement
Christ taking away the sin of our holy things

schools.summary: Gill: the month Nisan or Abib, made the first month upon Israel's coming out of Egypt. Henry: the appointment of the Passover sacrifices, not the paschal lamb itself but the public offerings of the feast.

themes: in the fourteenth day of the first month is the passover of the LORD
the feast of redemption heading the year
Christ our passover sacrificed for us (1 Cor. 5:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the feast not of the passover (the day before) but of unleavened bread, beginning the fifteenth and lasting seven days.
Keil & Delitzsch: in the fifteenth day is the feast; seven days shall unleavened bread be eaten.

themes: in the fifteenth day of this month is the feast: seven days shall unleavened bread be eaten
the purity befitting the redeemed
keep the feast with sincerity and truth (1 Cor. 5:8)

schools.summary: Gill: the first of the seven days was kept in a very religious manner, with no servile work done on it.

themes: In the first day shall be an holy convocation; ye shall do no manner of servile work
the day given wholly to God
a holy rest figuring the rest of the redeemed

schools.summary: Gill: the sacrifice was of the creatures next mentioned, two young bullocks, one ram, seven lambs, all without blemish.

themes: two young bullocks, and one ram, and seven lambs... without blemish
the rich burnt offering of the feast
the unblemished Lamb, Christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19)

schools.summary: Gill: the quantity of flour for a bullock, a ram, and a lamb is the same as in the foregoing sacrifices, mingled with oil.

themes: their meat offering shall be of flour mingled with oil... three tenth deals... for a bullock
proportioned as the other offerings
the fruit of the field joined to the blood of the lamb

schools.summary: Gill: notwithstanding all their services and sacrifices, and though the day was a holy convocation, there was need of a sin offering to expiate their guilt, typical of Christ who takes away the sins of our holy things; it was of the kind that were eaten.

themes: one goat for a sin offering, to make an atonement for you
the holiest day needing atonement
Christ taking away the sin of our holy things

schools.summary: Gill: the feast offerings were beside the daily morning sacrifice, and also the daily evening, though not expressed.
Wesley: the morning sacrifice alone is mentioned because the feast began with it, and chiefly because this alone was doubtful whether it should be offered before the festal sacrifices.

themes: Ye shall offer these beside the burnt offering in the morning... a continual burnt offering
the feast added to the daily lamb
constant worship the ground of every solemnity

schools.summary: Gill: the same — two bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs — were offered every one of the seven days, though only the first and last were holy convocations.
Keil & Delitzsch: the daily offering of a sweet savour throughout the seven days, beside the continual burnt offering.

themes: After this manner ye shall offer daily, throughout the seven days... of a sweet savour
the same offering each of the seven days
the constancy of devotion through the season

schools.summary: Gill: the seventh day was a holy convocation as the first, with no servile work done on it.

themes: on the seventh day ye shall have an holy convocation; ye shall do no servile work
the feast closed as it opened
framed with worship, entered and left in reverence

schools.summary: Gill: this was the day of Pentecost, fifty days from the bringing of the sheaf of the wave offering, when the firstfruits of the wheat harvest were brought.
Clarke: the feast of weeks and of Pentecost (Lev. 23:15).

themes: in the day of the firstfruits, when ye bring a new meat offering... after your weeks
the feast of weeks, Pentecost
fulfilled when the Spirit was poured out (Acts 2:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the burnt offering was of the same kind and number of creatures as on the first day of the month and the days of unleavened bread, with the same meat offering and a kid for a sin offering, a sweet savour unto the LORD.

themes: two young bullocks, one ram, seven lambs of the first year
the same offering as the other feasts
one ground of acceptance, drawing near through sacrifice

schools.summary: Gill: the kid of the goats for a sin offering, of the sort that were eaten, typical of Christ made an offering for sin; even the day of firstfruits required a sin offering to make atonement.

themes: And one kid of the goats, to make an atonement for you
atonement even on the day of firstfruits
the harvest joy resting on the atonement for our praises

schools.summary: Gill: the daily sacrifice of morning and evening, inculcated as not to be omitted in any festival, being so necessary and so eminent a type of the great sacrifice of the Messiah; the victims without blemish, with their drink offerings.

themes: Ye shall offer them beside the continual burnt offering... without blemish
the daily lamb never set aside
the eminent type of the great sacrifice of Christ
